Section 01
The Real Source of AR Breakdown

Accounts Receivable is rarely viewed as a strategic function — but when it breaks down, every part of the business feels it.

Most AR problems don't originate in collections. They begin much earlier — in broken processes, misaligned teams, and the absence of system-level controls. By the time an invoice reaches 90 days past due, the root cause is not a slow-paying customer. It is a failure of process, policy, and oversight that was never addressed at the source.

This playbook was built from direct, real-world observation of how receivables break down inside organizations. It identifies where issues begin, why they persist, and how to resolve them at the structural level — not just the transactional one.

The goal is not simply to collect money. It is to build the clarity, control, and consistency that makes payment predictable — before problems ever develop.

What follows is a practical, structured framework designed to move your AR function from reactive recovery to proactive control.

Section 02
Red Flags — Is Your AR Process Broken?

If any of the following conditions exist in your organization, your AR process is likely operating in a reactive, inconsistent, or uncontrolled state. These are not isolated symptoms — they are structural signals that require immediate attention.

⚠ No Credit Hold System

Customers continue receiving products or services regardless of outstanding balances — with no enforcement mechanism in place.

⚠ Billing on Past-Due Accounts

New invoices are issued on top of unresolved balances, compounding risk and creating confusion for both teams and customers.

⚠ Unapplied Cash & Credits

Payments exist in the system but are not properly applied, creating a distorted view of aging and account status.

⚠ No Cross-Functional Alignment

AR, Sales, and Operations operate in silos — without shared visibility, escalation structure, or collective accountability.

PROBLEM 01
No Credit Hold System

In many organizations, customers continue to receive products or services even after balances become significantly past due. Without a formal credit hold policy, there is no enforcement mechanism — and no structured point at which risk is evaluated or service is paused.

Impact Areas Cash Flow Exposure| Risk Accumulation| Customer Payment Behavior

Without a credit hold policy, there is no enforcement mechanism — and balances are allowed to grow well beyond the point of easy resolution.

How to Implement This
  • 1
    Define Thresholds

    30 days: reminder communication  ·  60 days: escalation review  ·  90 days: credit hold, pending exhaustion of collection efforts.

  • 2
    Assign Ownership

    AR monitors aging weekly. Sales is notified at 60+ days. Finance or management approves all credit hold decisions.

  • 3
    System Triggers

    Use your ERP or reporting tools to flag accounts automatically and generate a weekly aging review report.

  • 4
    Exception Process

    Document all approved exceptions. Require written leadership approval before proceeding with any override.

💡
Real-World Insight

In many organizations, past-due balances are treated as a collections issue rather than a control issue. Accounts move from 30 to 60 to 90+ days without any change in how the business engages with the customer — and without a defined point at which risk is formally evaluated or service is paused.

By the time action is taken, the issue is no longer about collecting a single invoice. It is about resolving a pattern that was never addressed early.

What This Reveals
  • Delayed enforcement significantly increases financial exposure
  • Customers are not incentivized to prioritize payment when no consequences exist
  • AR is forced into reactive recovery instead of proactive control
PROBLEM 02
Continued Billing on Past-Due Accounts

When billing continues without first addressing outstanding balances, the problem compounds over time. New invoices layer on top of unresolved ones, creating confusion for both the business and the customer — and making the eventual resolution significantly more difficult.

Impact Areas Invoice Clarity| Payment Prioritization| Customer Trust
What Should Happen Instead
  • Billing and AR processes operate in alignment, not in isolation
  • Accounts are reviewed for outstanding balances before new invoices are issued
  • Past-due balances are addressed before further billing proceeds
How to Implement This
  • 1
    Define Billing Controls

    Accounts over 60 days are flagged for review. Accounts over 90 days require billing restriction or explicit management approval before new invoices are issued.

  • 2
    Pre-Billing Review

    Before issuing any new invoice: verify aging status, identify outstanding balances, and confirm no active disputes or unresolved issues remain open.

  • 3
    Align AR, Billing & Sales

    AR provides aging visibility. Billing confirms account status before invoicing. Sales is notified when accounts are classified as at-risk.

  • 4
    Exception Handling

    Permit continued billing only with documented management approval. Track all exceptions for accountability and future pattern review.

💡
Real-World Insight

In many environments, billing continues as a standard operational function — without reference to a customer's outstanding balance. Invoices are generated even when prior invoices remain unpaid, creating layered balances that are harder to interpret and harder to collect.

From the customer's perspective, there is no clear signal that action is required. From the business's perspective, risk accumulates quietly — until AR is left managing a compounded balance built on unresolved activity.

What This Reveals
  • Billing without review actively increases financial exposure
  • Customers receive no prompt to prioritize payment resolution
  • AR is forced to manage compounded balances rather than isolated issues
PROBLEM 03
Unapplied Cash & Credits

Unapplied cash and open credits create a distorted view of receivables. Accounts may appear past due when payments already exist in the system — simply not yet applied. This leads to inaccurate aging reports, unnecessary customer outreach, and decisions made on flawed data.

Impact Areas Data Integrity| Reporting Accuracy| Customer Communication| Operational Efficiency

Over time, this erodes trust in the data itself — and decisions are only as reliable as the information behind them.

How to Implement This
  • 1
    Define Application Timelines

    Payments applied within 24–48 hours of receipt. Credits reviewed and cleared on a defined weekly schedule.

  • 2
    Assign Clear Ownership

    AR owns the cash application process end to end. Accountability for unresolved items is clearly documented and tracked.

  • 3
    Weekly Review Process

    Review the unapplied cash report weekly. Investigate missing remittance details. Clear all aged unapplied items on schedule.

  • 4
    Improve Remittance Visibility

    Standardize how payment details are received and processed. Ensure invoices align with customer payment references.

  • 5
    Track Aging of Unapplied Cash

    Monitor how long cash sits unapplied. Escalate any items that exceed the defined application timeframe.

💡
Real-World Insight

In many environments, unapplied cash is treated as a secondary task rather than an operational priority. Payments are received but sit unconnected in the system — creating situations where accounts appear past due despite payment, customers are contacted unnecessarily, and staff time is spent researching rather than resolving.

What This Reveals
  • Data accuracy is as operationally critical as collections activity
  • Poor cash application creates unnecessary rework and erodes confidence in reporting
  • Decisions are only as reliable as the data supporting them
PROBLEM 04
Lack of Cross-Functional Alignment

AR, Sales, and Operations often operate independently — without shared accountability for receivables outcomes. Customers may continue receiving service while accounts are significantly past due, simply because no coordinated decision has been made to address it.

Impact Areas Accountability| Revenue Protection| Internal Communication

The result is not just delayed payment — it is mismanaged revenue flow driven by the absence of shared accountability.

How to Implement This
  • 1
    Shared Visibility

    Establish a centralized aging report accessible to Sales and Operations — not just AR. Everyone works from the same picture.

  • 2
    Define Escalation Points

    60+ days: notify Sales. 90+ days: cross-functional review of service continuation with leadership involvement.

  • 3
    Establish Ownership by Function

    AR manages aging and customer communication. Sales supports relationships and follow-up. Operations aligns service delivery with account status.

  • 4
    Create Communication Loops

    Weekly or biweekly AR review meetings to discuss high-risk accounts and align on next steps across all departments.

💡
Real-World Insight

In many environments, each team operates on its own priority — AR collects, Sales maintains relationships, Operations delivers service. Without alignment, these efforts conflict. Customers receive service without pressure to resolve balances, while AR is expected to collect without organizational support to enforce action. The issue is not effort. It is the absence of coordination.

What This Reveals
  • AR performance is directly influenced by the behavior of other departments
  • Revenue risk increases when teams operate without shared accountability
  • Collections become significantly harder when service continues without controlled checkpoints
PROBLEM 05
Reactive vs. Preventive AR Culture

In many organizations, AR operates in a reactive state — action is taken only after invoices become past due, aging reaches critical thresholds, or customers stop responding. This creates a recurring cycle where issues are addressed too late, balances grow too large, and collections become increasingly difficult.

The goal is not to chase payment. The goal is to build the structure that makes payment predictable — before problems require a response.

How to Implement This
  • 1
    Establish Early Controls

    Define payment terms clearly at the point of engagement. Require a payment method — ACH preferred — before service begins.

  • 2
    Monitor Proactively

    Conduct weekly aging reviews. Identify early signs of risk before they escalate into collections situations.

  • 3
    Set Escalation Before Issues Grow

    30 days: reminder  ·  60 days: escalation  ·  90 days: action — credit hold or leadership review.

  • 4
    Align Teams Around Prevention

    AR, Sales, and Operations share responsibility for early intervention — not just resolution after the fact.

  • 5
    Standardize All Processes

    Consistent workflows, clear ownership, and defined timelines eliminate ambiguity and create organizational accountability.

💡
Real-World Insight

In reactive environments, AR is brought in only after a problem has already developed. By that point, the balance is larger, the customer is less responsive, and the situation is far more complex. Effort increases — but effectiveness decreases. The difference between reactive and preventive AR is not effort. It is timing, structure, and the discipline to act before urgency forces a response.

What This Reveals
  • Timing is among the most critical variables in receivables management
  • Prevention reduces both workload and organizational risk
  • Strong systems reduce the need for aggressive or escalated collections
Section 08
Recommended AR Workflow

A strong AR process is not a single task — it is a connected, end-to-end workflow. Each stage directly influences the next. When structured correctly, AR becomes predictable, controlled, and scalable.

1 — Invoice Creation

Invoices are verified before being sent, payment terms are clearly defined, and billing is issued promptly. Errors at this stage create avoidable downstream problems.

2 — Payment Setup

Before issues arise, establish the payment method (ACH preferred), communicate expectations clearly, and ensure the customer understands all relevant terms.

3 — Monitoring & Aging Review

Accounts are actively monitored on a weekly basis. Past-due accounts are identified early and risk accounts are flagged before they escalate.

4 — Escalation Process

Defined escalation eliminates guesswork: 30 days triggers a reminder, 60 days notifies Sales, and 90 days initiates a formal review or credit hold. Clear triggers drive consistent action.

5 — Cash Application

Payments are processed within 24–48 hours. Unapplied cash is reviewed regularly and accounts reflect accurate balances at all times. Clean data enables better decisions.

6 — Issue Resolution

Disputes are handled efficiently: root cause identified, ownership assigned, and resolution tracked. The objective is not just to resolve — it is to prevent recurrence.

7 — Reporting & Optimization

Performance is measured, aging trends are reviewed, problem patterns are identified, and workflows are refined accordingly. This is where sustainable improvement is built.

Section 09
Key AR Metrics to Track

A strong AR process is defined by measurable performance — not activity alone. Metrics provide visibility into cash flow health, process effectiveness, risk exposure, and operational efficiency. Without them, AR remains reactive. With them, it becomes controlled and improvable.

01
Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)
What It Measures
Average days to collect payment after invoicing.
Why It Matters
Lower DSO = faster, healthier cash flow and more efficient collections.
02
Aging Distribution
What It Measures
Receivables broken down by time bucket: Current, 30, 60, 90+ days.
Why It Matters
High concentrations in 90+ days indicate process failure — not just late payment.
03
% Receivables Over 90 Days
What It Measures
The share of total AR that is significantly past due.
Why It Matters
A primary health indicator for identifying high-risk exposure in the portfolio.
04
Unapplied Cash Balance
What It Measures
Total cash received but not yet applied to invoices.
Why It Matters
A high balance signals a data integrity issue that distorts aging and reporting.
05
Invoice Accuracy Rate
What It Measures
Percentage of invoices issued without errors or disputes.
Why It Matters
Higher accuracy correlates directly with smoother collections and stronger cash flow.
06
Avg. Days to Resolve Disputes
What It Measures
Average time from dispute identification to full resolution.
Why It Matters
Faster resolution reflects a more responsive AR function and reduces cash flow delays.

What gets measured gets managed. Strong AR teams operate on data — not on assumptions, activity counts, or intuition.

Section 10
Building AR From Scratch

If your business does not yet have a structured AR process, the objective is not complexity — it is clarity and consistency. A simple structure implemented early prevents the far more difficult work of fixing a broken one later.

  • 1
    Set Clear Payment Terms

    Net 15 or Net 30, due dates clearly stated on every invoice, and late payment expectations communicated at the start of each relationship.

  • 2
    Require a Payment Method Early

    Before or at the time of service, collect ACH or card details, store them securely, and encourage automatic payment enrollment.

  • 3
    Invoice Immediately

    Bill as soon as service is delivered. Ensure accuracy before sending and include all required details. Delayed invoicing is delayed payment — by definition.

  • 4
    Monitor Aging Weekly

    Even with a small customer base, review aging every week. Identify past-due accounts early and address them immediately — small problems grow quickly when ignored.

  • 5
    Set Escalation Rules Early

    Establish consistent triggers from the start: 30 days — reminder; 60 days — escalation; 90 days — action, including potential service pause or leadership review.

  • 6
    Do Not Continue Service Without Review

    If an account becomes past due, evaluate before proceeding. Protect revenue — not just relationships.

Section 11
AR Audit Framework

Use this checklist to quickly assess the current health of your AR process. Each item represents a foundational control. Gaps here are your starting point for improvement.

  • Are invoices reviewed for accuracy before being sent?
  • Are payment terms defined and consistently enforced?
  • Is there a formal credit hold policy in place?
  • Are accounts reviewed at 30, 60, and 90-day intervals?
  • Is unapplied cash cleared on a regular, defined schedule?
  • Are AR, Sales, and Operations aligned on account status and escalation?
Section 12
Implementation Roadmap

Improving AR does not require rebuilding everything at once. The most effective approach is disciplined and sequential: identify the primary breakdown, implement foundational controls, and build consistency before adding complexity.

Step 1 — Identify the Primary Breakdown

Locate the largest single issue: aging at 60–90+ days, no escalation structure, continued billing on past-due accounts, or inaccurate balances from unapplied cash. Focus on one area first.

Step 2 — Implement a Core Control

Introduce one simple, enforceable control: a credit hold at 90 days, weekly aging reviews, cash application within 48 hours, or a pre-billing account review. Start small, execute consistently.

Step 3 — Establish Visibility

Create consistent visibility into AR performance. Review aging weekly, track key metrics (DSO, % over 90 days), and ensure high-risk accounts are identified before they escalate further.

Step 4 — Align Internal Teams

Share aging reports with Sales and Operations. Define escalation points and establish shared agreement on when service should continue or pause.

Step 5 — Build Consistency

Execute the basics with discipline — same process, same escalation timing, same review structure every week. Consistency creates organizational control.

Step 6 — Expand & Optimize

Once the foundation is stable, improve reporting, refine workflows, and introduce automation where appropriate. Optimization comes after structure — not in place of it.

Improvement in AR comes from changing the system — not simply increasing activity. Structure, applied consistently, is what creates lasting control.
